pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that a skill gap in a learner's existing knowledge base — not the quality of the instructor — is the most common explanation for why some students of the same program get results while others do not; an advanced program delivers no value to someone missing prerequisite foundations.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi claims that the specificity of instruction required is inversely correlated with the skill level of the recipient: a low-skill person needs every sub-step explained (e.g., what a browser is, how to type), while a high-skill person can execute from a single high-level directive. Entrepreneurs misidentify this as a motivation problem when it is actually a skill-gap problem.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi asserts that switching a gym from a $118/month unlimited membership to a $39/week (equivalent to $168/month) pricing structure, while simultaneously cutting session count from 38 to 23 per week and reducing session duration from 60 to 45 minutes, can swing net profit by at least $77,640/year even assuming 10% customer attrition from the price change.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi's early mentor John, who operated a tanning salon chain, observed that members who paid a $100 sign-up fee for a $10/month membership churned at near-zero rates, while members who paid only $19 down with $19/month churned at a substantially higher rate. This supports the principle that sign-up fee magnitude inversely predicts churn.
